dialogue.utterance: Unit 2, respond to unconscious female at 22nd and Broad with possible overdose or fainting episode.
Hi, I'm Jason, a paramedic with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today, sir?
She collapsed in the bathroom. I found her on the floor afterward. She wasn't breathing well and had a needle nearby.
Ma'am, can you squeeze my fingers for me? Ma'am, can you follow my finger with your eyes?
She's not responding. No eye opening observed.
No verbal response to my questions.
No motor response to pain or verbal cues. Unresponsive. GCS 3.
We need to manage her airway. She has agonal respirations.
Airways are not patent.
Respirations are 5 and agonal. No adventitious sounds.
Pulse is 89, regular and strong. Skin color is normal. Peripheral pulses are intact.
Pupils are pinpoint and non-reactive to light.
No signs of trauma. No rash or bruising noted.
Agonal respirations remain. I'm inserting a Nasal Pharyngeal Airway to assist breathing.
Ma'am's boyfriend, can you share what you saw before the collapse?
She came out of the bathroom, fell suddenly, and collapsed. I found a used needle in the bathroom. She uses heroin occasionally, but this is the first overdose I've seen.
Ma'am, is there a relevant medical history I should be aware of?
She has asthma and uses albuterol when needed. Also, bipolar disorder, managed with therapy only.
She hasn't displayed any known drug or food allergies.
Blood pressure is 113/95 mmHg. Holding steady for now.
Respiratory rate is 5 and shallow.
Pulse is 89, regular and strong.
SpO2 is 99% on ambient air.
EKG shows sinus rhythm. No issues noted so far.
Glucose is reading 0-tells us it's an opioid overdose.
Initiate opioid overdose protocol 7-1 with naloxone. Partner, get some Narcan.
Administering 2mg naloxone intranasal now.
Starting oxygen administration via BVM, 15 liters per minute.
Starting capnography to monitor airway and CO2 levels.
Making an attempt at IV access with the extremity vein-arm or leg. Using right antecubital.
Partner, she is now opening her eyes. That's E4 in GCS.
Her grasp to my finger is brief-M3. Can follow one simple command. Verbal is slurred-V2. GCS 9.
Respirations improved, now at 16, effective and non-labored.
Pulse is now elevated to 111 and regular.
Blood pressure has increased to 157/117 mmHg.
Current SpO2 is showing 0-probably artifact.
EKG now shows sinus tachycardia. No arrhythmias appear to be present.
Blood sugar is up to 225-no signs of hypoglycemia.
Attaching 4-lead ECG monitoring now per protocol.
Ma'am's boyfriend, did you see any signs of fainting or near-fainting prior to collapse?
No, she did not faint or appear faint. She just came out of the bathroom and fell suddenly.
She's agitated, keeps trying to pull off the oxygen and restraints-administering 2.5mg midazolam intramuscular now. In the left upper deltoid.
She remained combative and tried to break free from the restraints. I can roll her onto the stretcher now and apply all straps to stabilize.
Re-positioning her to semi-Fowler's for optimal airway and oxygenation during transport.
Transporting cold with no changes in her condition noted during the lowering down the 3 flights of stairs.
Patient is fully secured with all straps in place. She is agitated but stable for transport.
[COMPANY_NAME], have a behavioral health team ready. This is a combative opioid overdose patient-current GCS 9, EKG shows sinus tachycardia, and she is agitated and combative.
